dialogue.utterance: Hello! I'm Ryan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What prompted you to call 911 today?
I've been throwing up all evening and my stomach hurts really bad. It's the upper part mostly.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where we are? Just to start with something simple.
What is your name?
My name is Sarah.
What county are we in right now? How about time of day?
Your airway is clear and unobstructed based on your ability to speak and breath normally.
Your breathing looks even and unlabored to me. Any issues or discomfort there?
Pulse is 66. It's consistent and regular.
When did the vomiting and pain start, and what made you decide to call 911?
Have you had any prior abdominal issues or surgeries?
Do you have any drug, food, or substance allergies?
Are you taking any medications for any other condition currently?
Can you tell me how your day went before this happened? When was your last meal or drink?
How would you describe the pain? Is it cramping or something else?
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how bad is the pain now?
Does the pain in your upper abdomen get worse when you change positions, like sitting or lying down?
Let me check your stomach for tenderness. Could you lift your shirt up for a clearer look?
Your overall chest and back don't show any signs of distress or asymmetry.
We're applying four-lead EKG now. Initial reading shows sinus rhythm.
Your blood pressure is 148 over 87.
Your pulse is at 66 beats per minute.
Your breathing is at 16 per minute and unlabored.
Oxygen saturation of 100 percent. Good oxygenation.
Blood sugar is at 148. We'll check that again in a short while.
You're alert and oriented in all four areas. You're AAOx4.
We're setting up an IV in your left arm using an 18-gauge catheter with normal saline.
I'm giving 4 mg of Zofran to help with the nausea and vomiting. It may take effect within a few minutes.
Your abdomen is soft but tender in the upper area. The pain seems to get worse when you move.
You're reporting abdominal cramping with vomiting, nausea, and some greenish-yellow vomit.
You mentioned the vomit is greenish-yellow. Is that still the case right now?
We just obtained a 12-lead EKG and it also shows sinus rhythm without any acute changes.
Your blood pressure is now 157 over 80.
Still 66 beats per minute, no changes there.
Your breathing remains at 16 per minute, steady and unlabored.
Continued 100% oxygen saturation, no decline in any monitoring.
Blood sugar rechecked at 148 again. It's staying stable.
Starting 500 cc IV fluid bolus of normal saline for hydration support.
You've reported the pain as 7 on the 10-point scale, cramping in the upper abdomen, not radiating, and worsens with movement.
We're following Protocol 3-9 for medical nausea and vomiting. There's no indication of cardiac involvement.
You've chosen Chippenham ER as your destination. We'll be preparing for transport now.
You'll be placed on the stretcher for transport. There may be some ongoing vomiting during the ride, but you're doing well up to this point.
Your alertness and coordination remain consistent. We'll continue to monitor it as we move forward.
Cramping, nausea, and vomiting are still present. Vomitus continues to appear greenish-yellow.
How would you rate the pain now? Is the character the same as before or changed at all?
Second EKG reading also shows sinus rhythm with no acute findings.
Blood pressure now reads 157 over 80.
No changes in heart rate-still at 66.
Respirations continue at 16 per minute. You're breathing normally on your own.
O2 saturation is still stable at 100%, indicating no need for supplemental oxygen.
Blood sugar remains at 148. No significant change detected.
No changes in level of consciousness-your AAOx4 has been consistent.
Tender area in the upper abdomen remains the same. Moving still causes increased pain.
Still fully alert and responsive. No change in mental status observed.
Motor and response functions are consistent from the start of this process.
Reconfirmed at 16 respirations. Normal and unlabored.
Transport status and treatment updates made. IV line and fluids are being managed consistently.
The 500 cc bolus is complete. We're assessing IV site for potential irritation.
Destination to Chippenham remains unchanged.
Reassessing vomitus-greenish-yellow as previously observed.
Nausea, vomiting, and upper abdominal cramping still reported. No shock or new symptoms observed.
Has the pain remained localized and cramping? Does movement still increase it?
No fresh bleeding or additional symptoms have emerged at this time.
All conditions steady during preparation for handoff.
You've been placed on the stretcher. She adapted to the stretcher and transportation process well during movement.
ER nurse is ready for handover and has confirmed all necessary information from your file.
Dry mucosa observed as well as coldness reported by the patient. Vomitus noted as greenish-yellow in earlier descriptions.
Final BP read at 157 over 80.
Final heart rate at 66, no irregularities noted.
Final respiratory rate at 16, regular and unlabored.
Final 12-lead still shows nothing acute-consistent with prior reads.
O2 continues to be 100%.
Final glucose read is 148. No significant changes.
Final pain rating remains at 7/10 with localized cramping, no radiating pain, and worsened with movement.
No changes in destination, still set for Chippenham as planned.
Patient successfully handed off to hospital staff. No further action needed with status consistent.
Fully secure for movement to hospital. Patient status consistent with initial evaluation and transported without deviation from care plan.
Transportation goals achieved. Comfort and interventions given as outlined in records.
Comfort and intervention measures administered continuously during transport as outlined in medical notes for this case.
Final GCS remains at 15 (AAOx4) with no change during transport or arrival.
Patient's handling occurred smoothly despite vomiting episodes noted during transport as logs verified by EPCR documentation.
Adaptation to stretcher and bed was performed as reported, with all comfort conditions offered to ensure stability and reporting.
Hospital accepted care, with patient signing for herself and lack of insurance noted in logs and forms.
IV site and Zofran interventions continued without adverse reaction or issue as logged and observed by staff.
EKG, BP, and O2 all remained stable during and after transport.
Vomiting mitigation strategies were applied as per field protocols for safe transport and EPCR log requirements.
Patient was hospitalized and aware upon entry, with mild distress followed by full handover to staff.
Thing: EMT: Transfer to ER went smoothly and according to standard. Collaborated with hospital nurse for complete and accurate information exchange.
Post-transport documentation and logs were consistent with baseline field observations in combination with established EPCR entries.
Final AAOx4 remains consistent with all prior neurological evaluations in this case.
Patient remains alert and oriented on arrival and through all previous evaluations.
Route detour was necessary due to 95 SB closure, and the correction was administered and documented at Maury Street.
Mileage logs were reviewed with the hospital for transport documentation purposes as outlined in shift requirements and dispatch reporting.
No protocol deviations were noted; vomiting incidents were addressed with full field procedures, following Protocol 3-9.
Full documentation and preparation ensured ER compliance with all medical interventions and transfer conditions.
All logs were verified and entries matched with field and hospital documents for EPCR full traceability and compliance.
Incidents involving 54-year-old Female were fully transported under standard guidelines and EPCR outline matching Chippenham expectations.
Patient had clear choice of hospital and route, with no alternations aside from required detours documented and managed.
Patient decision was respected throughout care process under guidelines for adult autonomy selection at time of call and transport selection.
All field and hospital documentation was completed to ensure legal and procedure consistency. Everything noted and filed accordingly.
